output:
The MoE lane (Qwen3-30B-A3B) wins the serving-lane bakeoff with a clear 8.5× speed advantage over the dense 32B lane, as demonstrated by the 88 tok/s throughput versus 10 tok/s, while both models share the same memory footprint and cost within the unified-memory envelope.
Citations: [article_hermes_serving_lane_on_spark]

The patent-specialist post-mortem identified that the misdiagnoses were traced to a corpus audit that revealed 56 percent of rows leaking the synth pipeline's internal state into the training signal, which was the root cause of the fine-tune failure rather than the trainer itself.
Citations: [article_fine_tune_data_prep_decisions_on_spark]

The rerank step dominates latency, taking over 500 ms per query and outpacing the other retrieval modes.
Citations: [article_rerank_fusion_retrieval_on_spark]

The sandbox tax for NemoClaw on a single Spark is about 26 seconds of steady‑state overhead on top of the raw inference time, as shown in the side‑by‑side measurement of NemoClaw vs. OpenClaw on one DGX Spark.
Citations: [article_nemoclaw_vs_openclaw_dgx_spark]
The public context retrieved here does not provide information about that. Citations: []

The Kepler model card recommends the Q4_K_M variant because it is the smallest quantized version that still provides reasonable performance on consumer GPUs, with a file size of 3.8 GB and a throughput of about 31 tokens per second, which is suitable for chat applications where high‑stakes quantitative tasks are not required. Orionfold recommends the smallest quant because it balances disk usage and speed while maintaining acceptable accuracy for typical use cases, as indicated by its recommendation in the field‑note on Orionfold/finance‑chat‑GGUF on Spark.
Citations: [artifact_kepler_gguf]

Orionfold Arena is a single-screen cockpit for running, comparing, and scoring local language models on one NVIDIA DGX Spark, providing live telemetry, a leaderboard of published models, and a way to evaluate models that have been fine-tuned, quantized, or kept warm, turning a personal shelf of models into an instrument panel that can be used to fly. It is designed for solo builders who have accumulated local models and need a tool to drive them without leaving the machine.
Citations: [product_orionfold_arena]

The book argues that the gap is a trust gap, not a capability gap, because only 11% of organizations have achieved production deployment of AI agents, and organizations do not trust that agents will behave predictably, can be audited, will contain failures, and will withstand regulatory shifts.
Citations: [book_ch_09]

The Orionfold Arena cockpit is built and published through a monorepo that handles both building and publishing, with the BUILDER and PUBLISHER components operating on Spark and linking to external artifact hosts such as PyPI, Hugging Face, and GitHub. The static web preview is served via the MARKETER storefront, which provides a leak-proof static /arena/ preview and routes to the real hosts while maintaining a local-runnable CTA. This distribution pipeline is defined in the arena-distribution.md guide, which outlines the internal hop from the monorepo to orionfold.com and the external artifact hosts.
Citations: [doc_guides_arena_distribution_md]

Route: _GUIDES/arena-storefront-marketing.md because it explicitly governs storefront positioning and card/detail layout for orionfold.com, describing the distribution pipeline and CTA implementation.
Citations: [doc_guides_arena_storefront_marketing_md]

The Orionfold Local AI Workbench v1 specification defines Arena and fieldkit as the local AI Workbench product, describing them as part of a product track that bundles Arena, fieldkit, and surrounding Spark-local operator tools for building, evaluating, governing, and operating enterprise AI artifacts on NVIDIA hardware. This specification explicitly states that Arena and fieldkit are bundled together within the local AI Workbench product track.
Citations: [spec_orionfold_local_ai_workbench_v1]

Route: doc_fieldkit_docs_api_publish_md because it directly describes the publishing side of the Orionfold production line and the Hugging Face repository creation process.
Citations: [doc_fieldkit_docs_api_publish_md]
